attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis attends the antenatal clinic during her first trimester of pregnancy. She was diagnosed with cystic fibrosis in childhood and has been managed with pancreatic enzyme supplements, regular physiotherapy, and intermittent courses of antibiotics for pulmonary exacerbations.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of cystic fibrosis?
options: Defective sodium channels causing excessive sodium absorption
Overproduction of mucus by hyperplastic goblet cells
Autoimmune destruction of exocrine gland tissue
Impaired chloride transport through a defective transmembrane protein
Abnormal mucin glycoprotein structure leading to viscous secretions
attributes.explanation:
Cystic fibrosis is caused by mutations in the gene encoding the impaired chloride transport through a defective transmembrane protein. Specifically, the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein functions as a chloride channel in epithelial cell membranes. When CFTR function is impaired, chloride ions cannot be properly transported across cell membranes, which disrupts the movement of water and other electrolytes.

This defect leads to dehydrated, thick secretions in multiple organ systems, including the lungs, pancreas, intestines, and reproductive tract. In the lungs, the thick mucus obstructs airways and provides an environment for chronic bacterial infections. In the pancreas, blocked ducts prevent digestive enzymes from reaching the intestines.

The incorrect options can be explained:

  • Defective sodium channels causing excessive sodium absorption - While sodium transport is secondarily affected in cystic fibrosis, the primary defect involves chloride channels, not sodium channels.
  • Abnormal mucin glycoprotein structure leading to viscous secretions - The mucins themselves are normal in structure; the viscosity results from dehydration due to impaired ion transport.
  • Overproduction of mucus by hyperplastic goblet cells - The primary issue is not overproduction but rather abnormal consistency of secretions due to impaired hydration.
  • Autoimmune destruction of exocrine gland tissue - Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder, not an autoimmune condition.

The CFTR defect also affects sweat glands, but in a different way. In sweat glands, CFTR normally reabsorbs chloride from sweat as it moves through the duct. When CFTR is defective, chloride (and sodium) remain in the sweat, resulting in the characteristically salty sweat of cystic fibrosis patients, which forms the basis for the diagnostic sweat chloride test.
learningPoints: Cystic fibrosis is caused by mutations in the gene encoding the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator protein, which functions as a chloride channel.
Impaired chloride transport leads to dehydrated secretions in multiple organ systems, including the lungs, pancreas, and reproductive tract.
Sweat glands in cystic fibrosis patients produce sweat with elevated sodium and chloride concentrations, which is the basis for the diagnostic sweat test.
references: Nelson-Piercy C. Handbook of obstetric medicine. 6th ed. Boca Raton: CRC Press; 2021.
